response.justification: James Smith is a web and full-stack developer whose primary expertise lies in TypeScript, Node.js, and DLang — none of which are relevant to a C++-first routing tiling role. His C/C++ exposure is limited to a 6-month stint at National Instruments in 2014–2015 and a university thesis project, neither of which demonstrates the modern, production-grade C++ the role requires. While he has built web-based GIS data collection platforms (GISCollective, Green Map), these are far removed from the low-latency, high-throughput geospatial routing tile pipelines this position demands. There is no evidence of experience with navigation systems, routing tile generation, or the relevant distributed-systems concerns at the systems-programming level.
strengths: Geospatial domain exposure via GISCollective and Green Map GIS projects, providing some familiarity with geo-data concepts
Demonstrated experience with high-throughput distributed systems at Sociomantic (millions of HTTP requests/second, async I/O, low-latency design)
Strong software craftsmanship practices — TDD, CI/CD, trunk-based development — suggesting disciplined engineering habits
concerns: No meaningful modern C++ or Rust experience; primary languages are TypeScript/Node.js/DLang, which are not applicable to this role
GIS work is entirely web-based data collection, with no connection to routing tile generation, map data pipelines, or navigation accuracy systems
Over a decade of career trajectory points firmly toward web/full-stack development, making a pivot to systems-level C++ geospatial work an unrealistic near-term fit
